description:
src/lib.rs:31 — 'DOPPLER_TOKEN is stored as Zeroizing<String> and zeroed when this backend is dropped.' This is true but does not warrant the inferred claim that the token is 'cleared from memory'.

Specifically:
  - Zeroizing<String> only zeroes the String's heap buffer that this struct owns. Earlier copies (made by std::env::var, the OS env block, libc getenv internals) are not zeroed.
  - reqwest's bearer_auth() copies the token into the Authorization header. That header is serialized into a request buffer and ultimately into TLS plaintext. None of those copies are zeroed.
  - Linux-default behavior allows the kernel to swap memory pages to disk. Swapped pages are not zeroed.
  - HTTP/2 may keep the header table (HPACK dynamic table) populated across requests. reqwest does not expose any control over this.

A serious user reading the existing comment would conclude the token is cryptographically protected. It is not — Zeroizing only reduces residency time of one specific allocation.

Fix: rewrite the comment to say what is actually true: 'The DOPPLER_TOKEN copy held by this backend is stored in a Zeroizing<String> so its heap buffer is overwritten when the backend drops. Earlier copies (in environ, in reqwest's request buffers, in TLS plaintext) are out of scope and may persist in memory or be swapped to disk. This is not a defense against a process-memory attacker.'
title: Module doc 'cleared from memory' claim for DOPPLER_TOKEN is overstated
